Tafseer:
The eloquence of the addresses 'Muzzammil' and 'Muddaththir':
As we pointed out, 'Muddaththir' and 'Muzzammil' share the same meaning. 'Muddaththir' is derived from 'dithar', which refers to the garment or sheet that a sleeping person wraps over himself.
Remaining wrapped in a garment, as clarified in the previous Surah, is a sign of anxiety and deep concern. The visions and spiritual experiences the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) had at the beginning of his mission were themselves highly weighty; then, when he shared them with his family members and they began to mock him, his anxiety grew even more. In such a state, he mostly remained wrapped in a garment, isolated from people, just like any anxious person does. To remove his anxiety and grief, Allah Almighty addressed him with great affection as 'Muzzammil' and 'Muddaththir', so that the address itself would reassure him that the Generous Lord is well aware of his condition, and since He addressed him with such compassion, He would also remove his distress. Thus, in the previous Surah, addressing him as 'Muzzammil', He commanded him with the night prayer, which served both as a remedy for his anxiety and as preparation for the mission coming next.

The command to stand and deliver the general warning:
'Arise and warn' (qum fa-andhir) - This is the mission referred to in the previous Surah by the words: (Indeed, We will cast upon you a heavy word) (73:5)—meaning: gird up your loins, stand, and warn your nation with complete resolve and courage. Pay no attention to opposition, hostility, unfavorable circumstances, or the isolation of the environment. When you muster the courage to stand, Allah Almighty will make your path easy, and the means of support will be provided from the unseen. We pointed out in the previous Surah that 'We will cast upon you a heavy word' refers to this command of general warning given here. Generally, commentators have interpreted 'a heavy word' as the revelation itself, but revelation had already been descending before this, so what would be the meaning of saying: 'We will soon cast upon you a heavy word!' In contrast, this command of warning was undoubtedly a highly heavy command for you. To proclaim monotheism in the ears of the leaders of Mecca and Ta'if, and to do so with the claim that you are the Messenger of Allah, warning them that if they reject you they will face His punishment, was no easy task. It was natural for you to feel some hesitation regarding this heavy responsibility. Thus, initially you restricted your work to your close family members, and even among them, you proceeded with great caution, sharing only some of your experiences to assess their reaction, which turned out to be highly hostile. Consequently, you experienced intense anxiety during that period, as depicted by the words 'Muzzammil' and 'Muddaththir'. But the purpose for which Allah Almighty had sent you had to be realized; therefore, first (in Surah Al-Muzzammil) you were directed to prepare for this situation, and then in this Surah, you were commanded to gird up your loins and stand to deliver the general warning.

Proclaiming pure monotheism:
This is the first command of the warning mentioned above. Monotheism has always held the primary status in the call of the noble prophets—namely, proclaiming the majesty and uniqueness of Allah alone. Placing the object first in 'And your Lord glorify' (wa rabbaka fa-kabbir) establishes restriction: all those besides Allah who claim majesty, or whose majesty is claimed, are completely false; you should proclaim the majesty and greatness of your Lord alone. In a pre-Islamic society, this proclamation was equivalent to inviting war with the entire world, but since the foundation of religion rests on this statement, every prophet had to proclaim it without delay.

The meaning of 'thiyab': 'Thiyab' is the plural of 'thawb', which means garments, but it can also mean the hem of a garment, or—as established from classical Arabic—it is used metaphorically for what we describe as 'the garment of the heart'. Imru' al-Qays's famous verse states:
'And if some habit of mine has grieved you, then draw my garments of the heart from yours, and they will separate.'
In this verse, the commentators have interpreted 'garments' as the heart, which is appropriate when understood metaphorically as 'the garment of the heart'. Imru' al-Qays also wrote:
'The garments of the sons of Awf are pure and clean [i.e., their character is unstained].'
In the light of this meaning of 'thiyab', the verse means: proclaim the majesty and unity of your Lord with complete courage. Even if the opponents resort to slander and exhaust their efforts, do not allow even a single speck of the impurity of shirk to stain the garment of your heart. Note that the Qur'an has explicitly described the polytheists as impure and shirk as impurity. This direction was given because in the subsequent stages, the leaders of the Quraysh proposed that they would accept all your terms provided you also accept some status for their deities, but the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) rejected their proposal firmly, and Allah Almighty also directed him with great emphasis: monotheism is the foundation of religion, so do not accept any compromise in it:
(They wish that you would compromise, so they would compromise) (Al-Qalam, 68:9),
and several other verses point to this situation. Here too, there is a highly important warning of this nature. Addressing the Prophet, the polytheists are informed: shirk is an impurity, not even a single speck of which the Messenger of Allah will tolerate on his garment.

'Rujz', 'rijz', and 'rijs' are closely related in pronunciation and are nearly synonymous. They are used for the filth that evokes disgust and aversion. While they can refer to any kind of filth, here the term refers specifically to the filth of shirk, reinforcing the command: (And your clothing purify) (wa thiyabaka fa-tahhir). That is: to keep your garment clean from the specks of shirk, stay far away from its impurity.
This instruction was not given because there was any risk of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) falling into shirk. Just as he remained pure during the Islamic period, he remained completely free from any trace of shirk during the pre-Islamic period as well. The objective was solely to inform the polytheists of the stance of the warner sent to them regarding their religion of shirk, and the instructions with which he was sent by his Lord.

A grammatical issue and its solution:
'Mann' means bestowing favors, and also means cutting off or terminating. In Surah Al-Qalam, He says:
(And indeed, for you is a reward uninterrupted [ghayra mamnun]) (68:3) - meaning: continue the warning and communication constantly. Do not abandon this task, thinking that enough warning has been delivered. Stand firm on the command of your Lord until He directs otherwise.
'Tastakthir' is not in the jussive form [as the response to a prohibition would be]. Although some reciters have read it in the jussive, the established reading is with a dammah [indicative], and it should be interpreted accordingly. In this case, it is either a circumstantial clause (hal) or an independent sentence. In my view, it is a circumstantial clause. 
'Istikthar' is well known in two meanings: first, increasing something or desiring more; second, regarding something as much or excessive. For the first meaning, a precedent exists in: (I would have acquired much [lastakthartu] of good) (Al-A'raf, 7:188). For the second meaning, the lexicographers state:
('Istakthara-sh-shay' means: he regarded it as much or counted it as much).
In my view, it is used in this second meaning here. The meaning, as noted above, is: continue this duty of warning constantly, without ever assuming on your own that it has been performed sufficiently.
This instruction was given because the warning of a messenger is intended to establish the proof, and the divine law is that if a nation ignores the warning, He destroys them after a specified respite. This respite is for establishing the proof, and Allah Almighty alone knows how much respite a nation should receive. The messenger's duty is to continue his work until Allah Almighty directs him that his duty has been performed and he must migrate, leaving the nation to its destiny. If the messenger departs on his own, assuming his duty has been performed, there is a risk of miscalculating the situation, as occurred with Prophet Jonah (peace be upon him). He was corrected, and after passing through a severe trial, was sent back to his nation to warn them again, which resulted in their accepting faith.
To protect the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) from such haste, He gave this warning at the very beginning: continue your work, and do not assume on your own that the duty has been sufficiently performed. The meaning is: this decision belongs to Us, not to you:
(So be patient for the decision of your Lord and be not like the companion of the fish) (Al-Qalam, 68:48),
and other similar verses teach patience and firmness, which is also mentioned in the subsequent verse.
Our commentators have generally interpreted this to mean: do not bestow a favor upon anyone with the hope of receiving a greater favor in return. Although the words can accommodate this meaning, look at the context of the discourse. What relevance does such advice have here? I believe they committed a slip in determining the meaning of these two words. We have clarified their meaning, making the true import of the verse clear.

The meaning of this is identical to:
(And be patient for the decision of your Lord, for indeed, you are in Our eyes) (At-Tur, 52:48).
When 'sabr' (patience) is connected with the preposition 'li', it means waiting with patience and firmness.
Related to the preceding instruction, this commands: do not abandon your work at any stage, but continue in it with patience and firmness, waiting for the decision of your Lord. On this firmness depends your success and the establishment of the proof upon the nation.

The primary subject of the warning:
This refers to the primary subject of the warning, heedlessness of which is the root of all evils. The primary cause of the Quraysh's hostility to the Prophet's call was that they did not believe in the Resurrection. They regarded it as highly unlikely, and if they accepted it in some measure, they argued based on their worldly prosperity that they would receive better in the Hereafter, and if God seized them, their deities would save them through intercession. It is stated: warn them of this terrifying Day, for when the Trumpet is blown, that Day will be extremely difficult.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 8 and 9 was combined in the original text.]

'For the disbelievers, not easy' (ala-l-kafireena ghayru yaseer) - That is: they have regarded it as very easy, but for the disbelievers, this Day will not be easy.
Having stated this from the positive aspect, He repeats it from the negative aspect, mocking those foolish individuals who sat untroubled, assuming they would see what happens when the Day arrives. It is stated: it will not be an easy Day, but a highly difficult one. Whatever preparation can be made must be made today. Whoever fails to prepare today is seized forever, without any hope of escape.

Warning to the leaders of Mecca and Ta'if:
This is a highly severe warning to those arrogant leaders of Mecca and Ta'if who regarded their prosperity and ease as proof of their righteousness and being approved by God, expecting that if the Hereafter occurred, they would face no danger, but would receive even more than what they possess here. It is stated: leave the matter of these arrogant individuals to Us; We are sufficient to deal with them. Do not worry or feel anxious about them.
'Leave Me with the one I created alone' (dharnee wa man khalaqtu waheeda) - This is the exact style used in the same context in the previous Surah: (And leave Me and the deniers, possessors of ease, and allow them respite for a little) (73:11); we explained this style there.
'I created alone' points to the reality that when a human being is born, he brings neither wealth nor status, neither helpers nor followers. These things are granted by God's favor, and remain with him only as long as He wills. Their right is that the human should become more grateful, rather than assuming he is approved by God and will receive even more in the Hereafter. This is stated elsewhere as:
(And indeed, you have come to Us alone as We created you the first time) (Al-An'am, 6:94).
The meaning is: leave Me to deal with this arrogant person who came into the world alone like everyone else, but when We blessed him with wealth and status, he began to regard himself as a great entity, displaying such arrogance when warned of the Hereafter, asserting that he deserves even more there.
Pointing to a specific mindset, not a specific individual: A question arises here—is this pointing to a specific individual, or describing the general mindset of the wealthy of Mecca and Ta'if? Commentators have identified Walid bin al-Mughirah as the target. However, there is no proof for this restriction. This mindset was shared by all the leaders and wealthy of the Quraysh, and indeed, anyone who receives wealth and status falls into this delusion. Only a fortunate few are saved from this fitnah. Therefore, it is incorrect to assume these verses were revealed specifically about Walid bin al-Mughirah, though his character can be considered a prime example. We explained in the introduction that when the predecessors say a verse was revealed about someone, they mean his character serves as a prime example. This subject is explained in various places in the Qur'an to point to the general mindset of the rebellious, not a specific individual.
Note also that the relative pronoun 'man' (who/whom) is used here, which is used for both singular and plural, and the pronouns can likewise be singular or plural.

'And I made for him wealth extended' (wa ja'altu lahu malam-mamdooda) - That is: when he came into the world, he brought neither wealth nor children, but came bare and helpless like everyone else. It was Allah's favor that He blessed him with extended wealth. 'Extended wealth' means he possesses gardens, villas, herds of animals, estates, commercial establishments, and bank accounts in various countries; the modern capitalists can also be included in this category.

'And children present' (wa baneena shuhooda) - Along with wealth, Allah blessed him with sons who are present in every assembly, meeting, and battle, standing by him and assisting him in his extended business. It should be noted that in tribal life, family support and unity were of great importance, serving as the basis for defense and competition. Leadership was achieved by the one who possessed more sons and a larger family, with sons capable of standing by their father on every occasion. The word 'shuhood' points to this aspect.
'And I eased [life] for him easing' (wa mahhadtu lahu tamheeda) - That is: by blessing him with wealth and children, We made the path to honor, status, and leadership smooth and easy for him.

'Then he greedily desires that I should add [more]' (thumma yatma'u an azeed) - That is: the right of Allah's favor was that he should become grateful and obedient, but instead, he grew arrogant. When warned of a Day that will be extremely difficult for the ungrateful and disbelievers, he claims that if such a Day occurs, he will receive even more there.

Responding to the arrogant claimants:
This is a highly severe refutation of their vain assumption. It is stated: their dream will never be realized. Allah did not bless them with these things because they deserved them, or because of their own effort and wisdom, but solely as a trial to see whether they would remain grateful and obedient, or grow arrogant and rebellious, causing corruption on earth. This trial proved that they became enemies of Allah's signs. 'Signs' (ayat) refers to the Qur'an as a whole, and specifically to those verses that warn of the punishments of this world and the Hereafter, clarifying that He alone is the Lord and Protector in this world and the Hereafter.

The punishment for the ungrateful:
'Irhaq' means committing someone to a difficult task, and 'sa'ood' refers to a steep peak or climb that is extremely difficult to ascend.
This is the punishment for their ungratefulness, contrasted with: (And I eased life for him easing). Since they became worshippers of their own desires, failing to resist any temptation, they will be forced to ascend an extremely difficult climb in the Hereafter.
The difference in the nature of goodness and evil: Note that while Allah Almighty has endowed human beings with the capacity to distinguish between goodness and evil, He has also placed them in a trial—making the pleasures of evil immediate in this world, while its bitter consequences appear in the Hereafter; conversely, the difficulties of goodness are immediate in this world, while its reward appears in the Hereafter. Consequently, choosing goodness requires resisting desires at every step, which is like ascending a steep climb, whereas choosing evil offers immediate pleasure, while its consequence seems distant. This attraction draws the majority to the path of evil. Only those who possess patience and resolve can choose the path of goodness, which requires training. Jesus pointed to this: 'The gate of evil is wide and its path is easy, and many enter through it; but the gate of goodness is narrow and its path is difficult, and few find it.'
In Surah Al-Balad, He states:
(And We showed him the two ways. But he has not broken through the difficult pass. And what can make you know what is the difficult pass? It is the freeing of a slave, or feeding on a day of severe hunger an orphan of near relationship, or a needy person in misery, and then being among those who believed and advised one another to patience and advised one another to compassion.) (Al-Balad, 90:10-17).
The Qur'an has declared only those worthy of success in the Hereafter who show the courage to break through the difficult pass in this world. Those who fail to do so here will be forced to face the steep climbs of Hell, which they will be unable to ascend.

Depicting the hostility of the opponents:
In verse 16, He stated: (He was to Our verses stubborn); this portrays that hostility, and look at how complete a picture is drawn.
'Indeed, he thought and plotted' (innahu fakkara wa qaddar) - That is: when the Qur'an was recited to him, instead of expressing his reaction immediately, he took time to think, trying to convey to the onlookers that their leader is considering the matter seriously. 'ploted' (qaddar) - having considered the matter, he weighed the opinion in his mind to ensure he stated something that would convince others and make them declare it a highly wise opinion.

'So may he be destroyed for how he plotted! Then may he be destroyed for how he plotted!' (fa-qutila kayfa qaddar, thumma qutila kayfa qaddar) - May he be ruined for the wrong assessment he made! And may he be ruined for the baseless opinion he formed. Cursing him twice with emphasis before stating his opinion highlights the ugly nature of his opinion, and warns the listeners: when a person loses his intellect, he speaks incoherently like a madman, trying to prove precious gems to be worthless stones, while assuming himself to be a great planner.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 19 and 20 was combined in the original text.]

This portrays the arrogant manner he adopted while expressing his opinion. Pondering over it, the Qur'an has drawn such a complete picture that even if his actual opinion was not mentioned, an observer could easily understand what kind of opinion this manner reflects. It is stated: first, he raised his head from reflection to look at the people's faces, assessing their mood to see if the moment was suitable. Then, he frowned and made a face, letting the onlookers know from his expression that the opinion he formed after reflection is highly disappointing. Then, he turned back with extreme arrogance and walked away, whispering to himself.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 21 and 22 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 21, 22, and 23 was combined in the original text.]

'And he said, "This is not but magic imitated. This is not but the word of a human"' (fa-qala in hadha illa sihruny-yu'thar, in hadha illa qawlu-l-bashar) - Although the preceding depiction made the matter clear, the Qur'an also recorded his actual words to show what poison he uttered as he turned back with arrogance. It is stated: he said that this is merely magic handed down since ancient times, and is nothing but human speech.
Why the Qur'an was called magic: We have explained in various places that when they could not deny the matchless eloquence and impact of the Qur'an, they tried to counter its influence by calling it magic, to prevent the common people from being awed by its divine nature. Calling it magic, they wanted to convince people: 'The impact within this speech is not because it was revealed from heaven, but is merely linguistic and verbal magic, which is not new or unique to the Qur'an, but has been handed down since ancient times. There have been other poets and speakers in this land whose speech possessed this impact, but no one regarded them as messengers of God, nor did they claim to be such, and no one regarded their speech as divine; so why should this speech be given a divine status!'
'This is not but the word of a human' - That is: this is merely human speech. Give its magical style as much importance as you wish; we do not deny its eloquence, but do not try to give it a divine status.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 24 and 25 was combined in the original text.]

The end of the arrogant:
This describes the end of such arrogant individuals. It is stated: We will soon cast him into Hell.

'And what can make you know what is Hell?' (wa ma adraka ma saqar) - This expresses the terror of Hell, asking: 'What do you assume Hell is!' meaning: no one should regard it as a minor thing. Its terror cannot be estimated here; only those who face it will know its reality. Unfortunate is the one who lives his life heedless of it! This address is general, and there is no reason to restrict it to the Prophet.

'It lets not remain and leaves not' (la tubqee wa la tadhar) - 'Abqa 'alayh' means he showed mercy and pity. Although the preposition is not mentioned here—as there was no occasion for it—the verb is used in this sense. The meaning is: this Hell will be so tyrannical that it will neither show any mercy to reduce the punishment, nor will it ignore anyone so they can escape. There is neither hope for mercy nor for being overlooked. It will be completely merciless and fully alert!

'Bashar' means the skin of the body. That is: the heat of its flames will be such that it will ঝলসানো (scorch/burn) the skins of the criminals from a distance. Elsewhere, the words: (It peels away the skin of the head) (Al-Ma'arij, 70:16) appeared. These are the effects of the intense heat of Hell that will begin to affect the criminals before they even reach the actual place of punishment, showing that if the beginning is such, who can estimate the end!

Answering a question regarding the allegorical verses:
It is stated: over it are nineteen. The specific noun is not mentioned here, but the subsequent verse indicates they will be angels. Mentioning only the number here implies that their characteristics, appearance, and power will be perfectly suited for the duty they are assigned. The criminals will realize upon seeing them that they will show no mercy, and there is no hope of escaping their grasp.
Here, a question may arise: What is the wisdom behind the number nineteen, and if their number is nineteen, why was it necessary to mention it? This is answered in the subsequent verse, but first keep a necessary introduction in mind:
Specifically, while the Hereafter is proved by intellect, nature, and the signs of the universe, and the Qur'an has explained them fully, the details of Paradise and Hell belong to the category of 'mutashabihat' (allegorical verses), the actual reality of which is known only to Allah. He has explained them through parables and metaphors so that we can establish a basic concept in our minds, but we cannot comprehend their absolute reality. If a person tries to pry into their absolute reality, he falls into fitnah and may even deny the core reality proved by solid arguments. In such matters, the correct approach for a rational person is to believe in what Allah has told, knowing that the actual reality will manifest on the Day they appear before the eyes. This was explained in Surah Al-Imran:
(He is the One Who has revealed to you the Book; in it are verses [that are] precise—they are the foundation of the Book—and others allegorical. As for those in whose hearts is deviation, they follow that which is allegorical of it, seeking discord and seeking an interpretation. And no one knows its interpretation except Allah. And those firm in knowledge say, 'We believe in it. All [of it] is from our Lord.') (Al-Imran, 3:7).
Such deviated mindsets have existed in every age. Among the leaders of the Quraysh and the mischief-makers of the People of the Book, such people existed; therefore, when the Qur'an mentioned the nineteen angels, it also warned on the spot (as follows) that although the mischief-makers will use it for discord, Allah Almighty mentions such realities to increase the knowledge of those who seek it, and to expose the hidden corruption of those who seek discord.

The wisdom of mentioning the allegorical verses:
As noted, this is a timely warning. It is incorrect to assume this verse was revealed much later in the Madinese period; its connection to the preceding verse with a conjunction proves that they were revealed together. In contrast, verses revealed later and inserted next to earlier verses are clearly separated, as is the case in Surah Al-Muzzammil, verse 20, which is Madinese and is clearly separated from the preceding verse, without a conjunction. Here, it is connected with a conjunction, showing it was revealed together to inform the Prophet of the mindset of the mischief-makers: when they hear these things, they will mock them, but pay no attention to them. Allah Almighty mentions these things to increase the knowledge of the believers and to expose the corruption of those who seek discord.
'And We have not made the keepers of the Fire except angels, and We have not made their number except as a trial for those who disbelieve' (wa ma ja'alna as-haba-n-nari illa mala'ikatan wa ma ja'alna 'iddatahum illa fitnatal-lilladhina kafaru) - That is: just as Allah appointed only angels over all other departments of His universe, He appointed only angels over Hell. This shows that His every work is performed through His pure creation—the angels—so that nothing occurs contrary to His will. Thus, He appointed angels over the prison of Hell.
This also answers those mischief-makers who mocked: 'If there are angels in Hell with the criminals, what is there to fear? We will live there just as they live.' The meaning is: they can make such jokes if they wish, but they should remember that there is a vast difference between the prison guards and the prisoners. They will realize this when they face the prison of Hell and its angels.
Allah Almighty has established trials in every aspect of life: 'and We have not made their number except as a trial for those who disbelieve' - This points to the wisdom of mentioning the number: its mention is a trial for the deniers of the Resurrection, allowing them to say whatever they wish. It should be noted that Allah Almighty has established trials in every aspect of life to expose the hidden evils of the bad and the hidden virtues of the good. Without these trials, hidden potential cannot be highlighted, nor can the good and bad be distinguished. Thus, regarding the details of Paradise and Hell, He mentioned certain realities that became a trial for the deniers—such as when the Prophet shared his experiences of Miraj or when the Qur'an mentioned the tree of Zaqqum, which the opponents mocked. In Surah Al-Isra, He states:
(And We did not make the vision which We showed you except as a trial for the people, and [also] the accursed tree in the Qur'an) (Al-Isra, 17:60).
The wisdom behind allowing the devils of jinn and humans to create such discords against the truth is explained in the Qur'an. In Surah Al-Hajj, He states:
(That He may make what Satan throws a trial for those in whose hearts is disease and those whose hearts are hardened. And indeed, the wrongdoers are in extreme dissension. And that those who were given knowledge may know that it is the truth from your Lord...) (Al-Hajj, 22:53-54).
We discussed this in detail under Surah Al-Hajj; refer to it for details.
Similarly, when He mentioned the number nineteen, He warned that although the wicked will use it for discord, this test is necessary to expose the corruption of those with diseased hearts, and to strengthen the knowledge and faith of those who seek the truth. Narrations state that when the leaders of the Quraysh heard there are nineteen angels, they mocked: 'If there are only nineteen, we can easily deal with them. One person can handle this many, and another can handle the rest.'
'that those who were given the Scripture may be certain and those who believe may increase in faith' (li-yastayqina-lladhina ootu-l-kitaba wa yazdada-lladhina amanoo eemana) - 'Li' (that) is to express the result—showing that: this struggle between truth and falsehood will result in the sincere People of the Book attaining certainty, and the believers increasing in faith.
'Those who were given the Scripture' refers to the good among them. Those of them who sincerely believed in their scriptures had no room to mock such details, as similar matters existed in their own scriptures. Finding confirmation in the Qur'an, their certainty was strengthened. These were the very People of the Book who later accepted the Qur'an.
Imitating faith is weak: The believers increase in faith because the objections of the opponents are exposed to them, and they realize they are completely baseless. The true nature of a thing is understood best through its contrast. Having examined both sides, the believer chooses faith based on insight, not mere imitation; hence, his faith is firm, and every trial increases it, whereas mere imitating faith lacks deep roots and can be shaken by a minor wind.
'and those who were given the Scripture and the believers may not doubt' (wa la yartaba-lladhina ootu-l-kitaba wa-l-mu'minoon) - This states the same point from the negative aspect: Allah establishes this trial to protect the sincere People of the Book and the believers from the attacks of doubt, serving as a preparatory protection.
Praising the good among the People of the Book: Note that the good among the People of the Book are mentioned alongside the believers, although this Surah is from the early Meccan period when there was no direct interaction with them. Some have used this as an argument to declare this verse Madinese, but this is incorrect. The good among them were on the same path as the believers; therefore, Allah Almighty mentioned them at the very beginning to clarify their role in this new mission, their status in His sight, the trials they would face, and how they should benefit from the arrangements He made to preserve their faith.
The attitude of the envious Jews: 'and that those in whose hearts is disease and the disbelievers may say, "What does Allah intend by this as an example?"' (wa li-yaqoola-lladhina fi quloobihim-maraduw-wa-l-kafiruna mada arada-llahu bi-hadha mathala) - This describes the impact of these allegorical verses on the envious Jews and the disbelievers.
'Those in whose hearts is disease' is commonly interpreted as the hypocrites, assuming 'disease' (marad) is used only for hypocrisy. However, this is incorrect. We have clarified in this book that it is also used for envy and malice, and on such occasions, it refers to the Jews due to their envy of the Children of Ishmael and the Prophet. We discussed this in Surah Al-Baqarah. Here, their attitude is contrasted with that of the good People of the Book, showing that they and the polytheists of the Quraysh will adopt the same attitude, asking in a mocking manner: 'What does Allah intend by this as an example!'
We explained this in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 26—showing that they will ask in an arrogant manner, implying these details are useless, and since Allah does not speak uselessly, the person attributing them to Him is fabricating them.
'Thus Allah leaves astray whom He wills and guides whom He wills' (kadhalika yudillu-llahu man-yasha'u wa yahdee man-yasha') - That is: through such trials, Allah leaves astray those who deserve it and guides those who deserve it. Recall that Allah's will is subject to His wisdom: He leaves astray those who are unworthy of guidance, and guides those who seek it. In a similar context, Surah Al-Baqarah states:
(And as for those who believe, they know that it is the truth from their Lord. But as for those who disbelieve, they say, 'What did Allah intend by this as an example?' He misleads many thereby and guides many thereby. And He misleads not except the defiantly disobedient.) (Al-Baqarah, 2:26).
Dealing a blow to the pride of the critics: 'And none knows the soldiers of your Lord except Him' (wa ma ya'lamu junooda rabbika illa hu) - This deals a blow to the pride of those critics who assume they know all the secrets of the universe or possess the intellect to comprehend them. It is stated: this assumption is completely false. No one knows the host of your Lord except He alone. He alone knows how many His soldiers are, what their capabilities are, what weapons they possess, and where they are assigned. No one can comprehend this. When Allah Almighty informs of certain secrets, human beings should appreciate it as a valuable addition to their knowledge, rather than assuming the universe is limited only to what they have seen.
Indeed, the critics of this age have raised doubts regarding the Qur'an or tried to provide twisted interpretations due to this very pride of intellect, rejecting what exceeds their limited understanding.
'And it is not but a reminder to humanity' (wa ma hiya illa dhikra lil-bashar) - This states the purpose of these verses describing the conditions of the Resurrection: We did not reveal them for criticism, but to warn and remind people so that those who wish to be saved from the punishment may prepare before it arrives. Allah has communicated the warning; it is up to the people to benefit from it. Those who fail to benefit will have no excuse. The divine law is to clarify the good and bad; forcing anyone to accept guidance is contrary to His law.
Commentators have generally taken 'it' (hiya) as referring to 'Hell' (saqar), but in my view, it refers to the warning verses describing the conditions of Hell, because the status of being a 'reminder' (dhikra) belongs to these verses, not to Hell itself.

Referencing some signs of the universe:
Swearing by some signs of the universe, it is stated: the Resurrection and Hell of which people are warned are among the great events of this universe, and their occurrence is certain; but Allah's works are subject to a specified plan, and they will manifest at their appointed time. Assuming they will not occur because they are delayed is foolishness. If someone hastens the morning during the night, the morning will not appear before its time. The same is the case with the Resurrection: its time is fixed, and it will manifest exactly at that time.
'Nay, by the moon' (kalla wa-l-qamar) - First, an oath is sworn by the moon, preceded by 'kalla' (nay) to refute the deniers' assumption with force. We explained the use of 'kalla' before an oath—showing that the speaker refutes the listener's assumption immediately, before stating the proof, and then swears the oath to confirm his statement.
Oaths in the Qur'an, as we repeatedly explain, are sworn as testimony and proof of the claim coming after them. Here, the addressees are the deniers of the Resurrection, and the doubt under discussion is: if the Resurrection is to occur, why is it delayed? The moon is presented as an example: just as it passes through various phases from habelle to the full moon, similarly you will also pass through various stages until you reach the station of which you are informed, witnessing the perfect manifestation of your Lord's justice. Just as the moon has specified phases:
(And the moon—We have determined for it phases) (Ya-Sin, 36:39),
similarly the Day of Recompense has specified phases through which it must pass before manifesting. Every change in the universe is subject to Allah's plan, and no one's desire or haste can affect it. This Day is bound to occur to fulfill the purpose of creation, but it will manifest exactly at its appointed time, without a single second's difference.
The Qur'an has used the moon's rising, setting, and phases to argue for various religious truths, and we will explain them. Here, the aspect of gradual development is kept in view to warn those who are hastening the Resurrection: every sign of Allah has a specified program based on wisdom. The manifestation of His justice is certain, but will occur at its appointed time. In Surah Al-Inshiqaq, an oath is sworn by the moon for a similar purpose: 
(And [by] the moon when it becomes full, you will surely embark upon state after state) (Al-Inshiqaq, 84:18-19).
That is: your presentation before the Lord's court for recompense is certain, but just as the moon reaches its full state gradually, this Day will also manifest after passing through its stages.

'And by the night when it departs, and by the morning when it brightens' (wa-l-layli idh adbar, wa-s-subhi idha asfar) - Following the moon, an oath is sworn by the night when it departs and by the morning when it brightens—meaning: just as during the darkness of the night, there is no sign of the morning, yet the morning eventually appears, similarly the Resurrection will manifest. This world is like the night whose darkness covers the morning of the Resurrection, but this darkness will eventually vanish and the Resurrection will appear suddenly. The God Who displays the light of day after the darkness of night daily, without anyone being amazed by this change, will likewise bring the Resurrection, and everyone will see what they regarded as impossible.
A similar oath appeared in Surah At-Takwir for the same purpose:
(And by the night when it departs, and by the morning when it breathes) (At-Takwir, 81:17-18).
Every corner of the universe contains a proof of the Resurrection: We have explained that this world is structured in such a manner that a rehearsal of the Resurrection occurs daily in every corner, providing a believer with a clear example to satisfy his heart, provided he seeks the truth.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 33, 34, and 35 was combined in the original text.]

This is the subject of the oaths: these verses warning of the terror of Hell are not a joke, but are among the great events and warnings of this universe. Unfortunate are those who mock them instead of learning a lesson.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 35 and 36 was combined in the original text.]

Benefiting from the proofs is up to the people themselves:
That is: warning people of this terrifying Day before it arrives was necessary so that no one could claim they were not informed. Thus, Allah Almighty established His proof through His Book. Accepting or rejecting it is up to the people themselves: whoever seeks success will accept it, and whoever is doomed to ruin will turn away with arrogance.

Deeds will bring salvation or ruin:
That is, the divine law is that just as an effect is bound to its cause, everyone is bound to their deeds. On the Day of Recompense, a person's deeds will save him or ruin him. If anyone assumes that lineage or intercession will save him, he should know that nothing of this sort will benefit him on that Day.

The people of the right hand:
On that Day, success belongs only to the people of the right hand (as-hab-ul-yameen). Their details appeared in their relevant place—referring to those who lived their lives keeping the Hereafter in view, and whose records will be given in their right hands. It is stated: they will receive a full reward, and Allah will bless them with His special favor. They will be in gardens, questioning one another about the end of the criminals they knew in the world.
The nature of this questioning is clarified in Surah As-Saffat, showing how they will approach the criminals in Hell and ask them about the cause of their end:
(And they will approach one another, questioning. A speaker among them will say, 'Indeed, I had a companion [in the world] who would say, "Are you indeed of those who believe? That when we have died and become dust and bones, we will indeed be recompensed?"' He will say, 'Would you [care to] look?' And he will look and see him in the midst of Hell.) (As-Saffat, 37:50-55).
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 39, 40, 41, and 42 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 39, 40, 41, and 42 was combined in the original text.]
That is, the people of Paradise, while questioning one another, will also address the people of Hell, asking: 'What led you into Hell?'

The confession of the people of Hell:
The people of Hell will confess that their own deeds led them to this end: the fault lies entirely with them, not with anyone else.
First, they will confess: 'We were not of those who prayed.' It should noted that just as monotheism holds the primary status among beliefs, prayer holds the primary status among deeds. Every prophet called to this deed first, and made religion dependent on it.
Next, they will confess their failure to spend: 'We did not feed the poor.' After prayer, the second status belongs to spending (zakat), and the foundation of all righteous deeds rests on these two. We have explained that prayer connects the servant with the Creator, while spending connects him with His creation, and on these two depends the stability of religion.
Next, they will confess that they used to mock and raise doubts regarding the Resurrection just as others did, until death arrived and gave them absolute certainty of the realities they used to deny.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 43, 44, 45, 46, and 47 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 43, 44, 45, 46, and 47 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 43, 44, 45, 46, and 47 was combined in the original text.]

'Until there came to us the certainty' (hatta atana-l-yaqeen) - 'Yaqeen' means certainty, but since after death all realities become visible and a person is forced to believe in them, death is described here as certainty.
These confessions are recorded to prove: (Every soul, for what it has earned, will be retained), so that those foolish individuals who mock the Resurrection based on lineage or intercession may open their eyes and realize from the statements of their associates that nothing but righteous deeds will benefit them before Allah.

Nullifying the polytheists' assumed intercession:
This nullifies their assumed intercessors and their intercession: there will be neither intercessors nor intercession.
This uses the Arabic style where 'negating a thing negates its necessity'. Imru' al-Qays, describing a desert path, wrote: 'It is not guided by its marker.' This obviously means there are no markers in it to guide. Regarding their assumed intercessors, the Qur'an explicitly states:
(They are not but [mere] names you have named, you and your forefathers, for which Allah has sent down no authority. They follow not except assumption) (An-Najm, 53:23).

Amazement at the condition of those who turn away:
This expresses amazement at their condition: they should have been grateful to their Lord for revealing a Book and sending a Messenger to warn them before the Day of Recompense arrived, but their condition is strange—they are turning away from this reminder.
'Turning away' (mu'rideen) is a circumstantial clause (hal) derived from the pronoun of 'them' (anhum), which is a recognized usage.

This portrays their panic: they are like frightened donkeys fleeing from a lion. 'Qaswarah' means a lion. When wild donkeys hear the roar of a lion, they flee in panic and are difficult to calm down; any minor sound makes them run as if the lion has appeared. It is stated: similarly, these people are so terrified of this reminder that they refuse to listen to even a single word.
It should noted that when a reality is so clear that a person cannot satisfy his heart by denying it, yet is not ready to accept it, his flight from it is like this. His desire is to avoid hearing any voice that might remind him of this reality.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 50 and 51 was combined in the original text.]

Their excuse for turning away:
That is: they have fabricated the excuse: 'If God had to reveal a Book, why was it revealed to him [Muhammad]? Why were we not given open pages directly!' as if this was difficult for Allah! This appeared elsewhere as:
(And when a sign comes to them, they say, 'We will never believe until we are given like what was given to the messengers of Allah.' Allah is most knowing of where He places His message) (Al-An'am, 6:124).

The actual cause:
That is: this is not going to happen, nor is there any need for it. The cause of their flight is not what they claim, but that they do not expect the Hereafter. 'Yakhafuna' here implies 'yarjuna' (to expect/look forward to), which is a recognized usage. The meaning is: since they are untroubled by the Hereafter and their desire is to remain heedless of it so their pleasures are not disturbed, no sign can convince them. Even if they were given open pages as they demand, they would still fabricate some excuse.

Comfort for the Prophet:
This shows indifference to these stubborn individuals and comforts the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). The meaning is: there is no need to please these stubborn individuals. This is a reminder; whoever wishes may benefit from it and choose the straight path. If they do not benefit, they will face the consequence; they will harm neither Allah nor the Messenger. The divine law is to warn people before seizing them so they have no excuse, and the Prophet's duty is to communicate this warning clearly. Placing guidance in their hearts is neither the Prophet's responsibility, nor is it Allah's law to force guidance upon those who loathe it.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 54 and 55 was combined in the original text.]

The divine law regarding guidance:
This points to the divine law regarding guidance: Allah Almighty blesses with guidance only those who use their hearing, sight, and heart, appreciate the light of nature, reflect on the signs of the universe, and keep their ears open to listen to the truth even if it is contrary to their desires. His will is subject to His wisdom: He blesses with guidance only those who appreciate it, and deprives those of their natural capacity who show disregard.
'He is worthy of fear and worthy of forgiveness' (huwa ahlu-t-taqwa wa ahlu-l-maghfirah) - Commentators have taken 'huwa' as referring to 'Allah', but in our view, it refers to 'whoever' in: 'so whoever wills may benefit'. 'Man' is used for both singular and plural; therefore, both singular and plural pronouns can be used. This explains who will benefit from this Qur'an and become worthy of Allah's forgiveness: those who possess fear of God will benefit from it and become worthy of His forgiveness, while those whose hearts are devoid of His fear will remain deprived of both. We explained under Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 2: (a guidance for those who fear God) who benefits from the Qur'an and who remains deprived; refer to it for details. There are various ranks of 'taqwa' (fear of God). The natural 'taqwa' endowed within human nature, which is mentioned in: (And inspired it [with discernment of] its wickedness and its righteousness) (Ash-Shams, 91:8), is what is meant here. Its complete explanation will appear in the next Surah.
